exemplar.title: Investigating the theory of reconstructive memory by performing a replication of the Loftus & Palmer (1974) study

detailedExemplarData.title: Investigating the theory of reconstructive memory by performing a replication of the Loftus & Palmer (1974) study

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Aim clearly stated and relevance to memory theory explained
Theory/model described with a clear link to planned investigation
Hypothesis operationalises both IV and DV precisely
weaknesses: Minor phrasing issues in aim statement
Slight digression into dual-process theory in background
Unclear LaTeX notation could confuse readers
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The introduction is well constructed, with a precise aim, a coherent theoretical framework and a fully operationalised hypothesis. However, minor issues in phrasing, a small theoretical digression and an unclear symbol prevent it from achieving the top mark band. A score of 4/6 reflects solid achievement with room for tightening and clarification.

strengths: Research design is well explained, with a strong rationale for independent measures
Sampling technique justified with clear inclusion/exclusion criteria
Controlled variables and materials explained in terms of validity
weaknesses: Procedure order (e.g. group exit sequence) lacks justification
Random-allocation method could be more rigorously concealed
Justification of some controls could be more concise
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration section demonstrates a clear, well-justified design, sampling and control of variables, comfortably in the upper band. Minor gaps in procedural justification and bias prevention mean it does not reach the maximum. A score of 3/4 accurately reflects strong performance with minor omissions.

strengths: Appropriate use of descriptive statistics and justified non-parametric test
Graph directly addresses hypothesis with clear labels
Findings are interpreted and linked to hypothesis
weaknesses: No test of distribution assumptions reported
Error bars are unspecified (SE vs SD)
Some interpretative claims lack empirical support or effect-size reporting
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Analysis applies the correct statistical techniques and links results to theory, but minor methodological omissions (assumption checks, effect-size, error-bar clarity) and small presentation errors limit its mark. A score of 3/6 reflects competent data handling with scope for more rigorous reporting.

strengths: Discussion ties findings back to reconstructive memory and compares with Loftus & Palmer
Strengths and limitations are relevant and explained in terms of validity
Modifications are logical and address limitations
weaknesses: Discussion is somewhat brief and occasionally repetitive
Modifications lack detailed justification (e.g. piloting protocols)
Some limitations could be more directly linked to specific validity threats
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation shows a reasoned discussion grounded in theory, with clear strengths, limitations and suggested modifications. However, brevity in discussion, some repetition and shallow justification of changes keep it from the top band. A score of 3/6 accurately represents a well-argued but not fully exhaustive evaluation.

strandSelections.reasoning: A bar graph with error bars is provided and clearly contrasts the two conditions, directly addressing the hypothesis.  Axis labels and numerical values are present, but titles/units are somewhat small and the source of error bars is not specified.  These minor faults place it in the âgraph addresses the hypothesis but contains errorsâ category, meriting 4.

The student explicitly states the p-value, rejects H0, and interprets the direction of the finding (higher estimates in the âsmashedâ condition).  Interpretation is connected to the descriptive statistics and supported by theory, addressing the hypothesis directly.  Minor stylistic issues keep it at 5 rather than 6, but the work meets all criteria of the top band.

The hypothesis lists both a null and a directional alternative, explicitly operationalising the IV (verb âsmashedâ vs âcontactedâ) and the DV (speed estimate **in kilometres per hour**).  The wording makes the predicted direction of effect unmistakable.  Because all elements are precise and fully operationalised, the strand earns the maximum mark in the 5-6 band.

Sampling is identified as convenience; inclusion/exclusion criteria (English proficiency, no psychology, no driving experience) are provided and **the choice is justified in terms of availability and cultural diversification**.  The explanation of how these factors influence generalisability fulfils the descriptor for 3-4 marks.

Reconstructive memory and schema theory are **described with supporting citations**; the student explains how schemas can âfill in gapsâ under System 1 processing.  The link to the study is then made explicit: verb intensity may alter the reconstruction of the accident.  While the theoretical section wanders slightly into dual-process theory, the **connection between theory and planned investigation is still clear and accurate**, fulfilling the requirements of the top band albeit with minor digressions that merit 5 rather than 6.

The discussion links the observed effect to reconstructive memory and Type 1 processing, compares findings to Loftus & Palmer, and comments on temporal validity.  Although brief, these comments go beyond description and constitute **a reasoned discussion with theoretical integration**, fitting the 5-6 band at a score of 5.

The aim â âto investigate the relationship between the intensity of the verb and memory for speed estimatesâ â is **clearly stated in a single, precise sentence** and is explicitly tied to a replication of Loftus & Palmer. The student then links this aim to the broader relevance of updating temporal validity and widening the cultural sample, thereby **explaining why the investigation matters**.  Minor phrasing issues exist, but they do not detract from an otherwise clear explanation, comfortably meeting the 5-6 descriptor.

Several strengths (random allocation, standardised procedure) and limitations (convenience sample, artificial task) are **explained in terms of how they affect validity and reliability**.  The coverage is relevant and analytical, meeting the criteria for explanation rather than mere listing, achieving a solid 5.

Controlled variables (setting, video, instructions) are listed and the student **explains why each control is important** for internal validity.  Materials are described with reasons (e.g., written instructions to avoid researcher bias).  This explanatory detail places the work firmly in the upper Exploration band.

Proposed modifications (counter-balancing in a delayed repeated study, using more videos, adding extra questions) are mentioned and each is **loosely linked** to a stated limitation, but the justifications are brief and lack depth (e.g., no detail on how additional videos would be selected or piloted).  This meets the 3-4 descriptor but is closer to the lower end; a score of 3 is appropriate.

The student outlines an **independent-measures design**, random allocation by drawing papers, and explains why this design minimises demand characteristics.  The rationale for avoiding repeated measures is also given.  This constitutes an explanation rather than a mere description, satisfying the upper band for Exploration.

Means, standard deviations and a Mann-Whitney U test are applied appropriately to two independent, non-parametric samples.  The choice of test is justified.  However, the treatment of an extreme value (153 km/h) is questionable and there is no evidence of testing assumptions beyond non-normality, so **minor inaccuracies remain**; hence the strand fits the 3-4 band with the top score of 4.

annotations.comment: The aim statement precisely names the replication study but would benefit from explicitly operationalising the DV here, rather than later.
The IV and DV are clearly stated and operationalised in the hypothesis, demonstrating precise measurement criteria.
The use of LaTeX \(\omega_{\rm{max}}\) here is unclear; remove or clarify this symbol to prevent confusion.
The independent-measures design is well justified, highlighting how it reduces demand characteristics and testing effects.
Exclusion criteria for drivers and psychology students are justified in terms of bias reduction and cultural relevance.
Random allocation via paper draw reduces bias but introduces potential allocation predictability; consider sealed envelopes for better concealment.
The bar graph addresses the hypothesis but should specify whether error bars represent SE or SD to avoid ambiguity.
The topic statement identifies reconstructive memory but could be refined by explicitly stating the specific aim and scope of this investigation to guide the reader.
The section header âAssessmentâ is very general; consider specifying this as the Psychology Internal Assessment to improve clarity.
The discussion of schema theory is well cited and explains how preexisting knowledge shapes recall, effectively linking theory to reconstructive memory.
The summary paragraph reiterates key points but lacks a clear connection to how this theory informs your chosen IV and DV; explicitly tie back to your variables.
The background study of Loftus & Palmer (1974) is described comprehensively, but including the sample size and design details would better ground your replication rationale.
Controls for environment, materials and instructions are explained clearly, demonstrating strong internal validity awareness.
The rationale for standardized instructions is well articulated, reducing researcher bias effectively.
Procedure is detailed step-by-step but lacks justification for the order of group testing; explain why group 2 leaves first.
Ethical procedure is described, but linking consent and debrief procedures to ethical guidelines (e.g., BPS code) would strengthen justification.
The table header contains a typo (âcondtionâ); correct this to maintain professionalism.
Interpretation linking the 2.48Ã difference in SD to semantic breadth is speculative; provide empirical support or temper this claim.
No normality test is reported; while non-parametric choice is justified, include a test of distribution assumptions for completeness.
Rationale for MannâWhitney U is clear, but reporting effect size (e.g., r) would enhance understanding of practical significance.
The description âsignificance level greater than p-valueâ is misleading; it should read âp-value less than Î± (0.05)â to align with convention.
Evaluation of random allocation strength is thorough and links to demand characteristic reduction effectively.
Proposed repeated-measures modification is relevant but lacks detail on counterbalancing and interval timing; expand your justification.
Conclusion restates findings accurately but could be strengthened by discussing broader implications for eyewitness testimony practice.
Discussion of ecological validity versus task artificiality is relevant, but the point is repeated twice; streamline to avoid redundancy.
In listing limitations, link each explicitly to its impact on validity and suggest how that limitation could be mitigated.
